Joints (Articulations)

A visual reference to fifteen articulations of the human body, organized by region with structural classification, range of motion, and clinical pearls.

15 Plates 3 Sections BIO 004

Section 1

Joints of the Skull and Vertebral Column

Articulations of the head and neck, from the temporomandibular joint to the repeating zygapophyseal joints of the spine.

Plate 01

Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ)

The hinge-and-glide joint between the temporal bone and the mandible, the most heavily used joint in the body during talking, chewing, and swallowing.

Bones
Temporal bone (mandibular fossa) and mandible (mandibular condyle)
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Hinge and planar
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Elevation, depression, protraction, retraction, lateral excursion
Clinical Pearl
TMJ dysfunction
TMJ dysfunction (TMD) affects up to 12 percent of adults, with women affected more often than men. Symptoms include jaw pain, clicking or popping with opening, headache, and limited mouth opening. Common contributors include nocturnal teeth grinding (bruxism), stress-related clenching, malocclusion, and inflammatory arthritis. Most cases are managed conservatively with night guards, physical therapy, and stress reduction.
Plate 02

Atlanto-occipital Joint

The condyloid synovial joint between the occipital bone and the first cervical vertebra, responsible for the "yes" nodding motion of the head.

Bones
Occipital condyles of the occipital bone and superior articular facets of C1 (atlas)
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Condyloid
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Flexion and extension (the "yes" head motion), lateral flexion, circumduction
Clinical Pearl
Internal decapitation
Atlanto-occipital dissociation, sometimes called internal decapitation, is a catastrophic injury where the ligaments connecting the skull to C1 are completely torn. It is often immediately fatal due to brainstem and high cervical cord injury. The injury most often results from high-energy trauma such as motor vehicle collisions, particularly in unrestrained children whose heads are proportionally larger than their bodies.
Plate 03

Atlanto-axial Joint

The pivot joint between C1 (atlas) and C2 (axis) that allows rotation of the head, the "no" shaking motion.

Bones
Anterior arch of C1 (atlas) with its facet for the dens, and the dens of C2 (axis)
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Pivot
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Rotation (the "no" head motion)
Clinical Pearl
Atlanto-axial instability in Down syndrome
Approximately 10 to 20 percent of children with Down syndrome have atlanto-axial instability due to congenital ligamentous laxity. The transverse ligament of the atlas, which holds the dens against the anterior arch of C1, is unusually lax. This puts the spinal cord at risk during contact sports or trauma. Current pediatric guidelines emphasize symptom-based evaluation rather than routine screening X-rays.
Plate 04

Zygapophyseal ("Z") Joint

The plane synovial joints between the articular processes of adjacent vertebrae, repeated throughout the spinal column.

Bones
Inferior articular facet of one vertebra with the superior articular facet of the vertebra below
Structural Classification
Synovial, plane
Functional Classification
Plane
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Gliding. Cumulative motion across many joints permits flexion, extension, lateral flexion, and limited rotation. Range varies by spinal region: rotation is greatest in the thoracic spine, and flexion is greatest in the lumbar spine.
Clinical Pearl
Facet syndrome
Facet (zygapophyseal) joint pain is a common cause of mechanical low back pain, especially in older adults with degenerative changes. Pain is typically axial (spine-centered) rather than radiating, and is often worse with extension and rotation. Diagnostic facet joint injections under fluoroscopy can confirm the source, and medial branch radiofrequency ablation provides longer-term relief in selected patients.
Section 2

Joints of the Pectoral Girdle and Upper Limb

The four joints linking the limb to the trunk, plus the radioulnar joints that produce pronation and supination.

Plate 05

Sternoclavicular (SC) Joint

The only true bony articulation between the upper limb and the axial skeleton, formed between the clavicle and the manubrium of the sternum.

Bones
Sternum (manubrium) and sternal end of the clavicle
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Saddle
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Elevation, depression, protraction, retraction, circumduction
Clinical Pearl
Posterior SC dislocation: the rare emergency
The SC joint is one of the most rarely dislocated joints, protected by strong sternoclavicular and costoclavicular ligaments. When it does dislocate, anterior dislocation is more common. Posterior dislocation is rare but dangerous: the medial clavicle can compress the trachea, esophagus, brachiocephalic vessels, or the lung apex. Posterior SC dislocation is a surgical emergency.
Plate 06

Acromioclavicular (AC) Joint

The articulation between the acromial end of the clavicle and the acromion of the scapula, completing the bony arch over the shoulder.

Bones
Acromial end of the clavicle and acromion of the scapula
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Planar
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Gliding
Clinical Pearl
AC separation: a contact-sport classic
AC joint sprains and separations are common in football, hockey, cycling, and martial arts, typically from a fall onto the point of the shoulder. The injury is graded by which ligaments are torn: type I (sprain only), type II (AC ligament torn), and types III through VI (progressive coracoclavicular ligament involvement with displacement). Most type I to III injuries are managed conservatively.
Plate 07

Manubriosternal Joint

The cartilaginous joint between the manubrium and the body of the sternum, marked externally by the palpable sternal angle.

Bones
Manubrium and body of the sternum
Structural Classification
Cartilaginous
Functional Classification
Symphysis
Movement Classification
Amphiarthrosis
Movements
Slight movement during respiration
Clinical Pearl
Sternal angle: the angle of Louis
The manubriosternal joint creates a palpable transverse ridge on the anterior chest known as the sternal angle (angle of Louis). It marks the level of the second costal cartilage, the T4 to T5 intervertebral disc, the bifurcation of the trachea (carina), and the start and end of the aortic arch. It is the single most useful surface landmark in chest examination.
Plate 08

Glenohumeral Joint

The shoulder joint proper. The most mobile joint in the body, sacrificing stability for an enormous range of motion.

Bones
Glenoid fossa (cavity) of the scapula and head of the humerus
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Ball and socket
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, rotation, circumduction
Clinical Pearl
The most frequently dislocated major joint
Anterior dislocations make up over 95 percent of glenohumeral dislocations. The shallow glenoid fossa, the weak anterior capsule, and the combined leverage of abduction and external rotation place the humeral head at risk anteriorly. Bankart lesions (avulsion of the anteroinferior labrum) and Hill-Sachs lesions (compression fracture of the posterolateral humeral head) are common findings on MRI.
Plate 09

Radioulnar Joint

Two synovial pivot joints, one proximal and one distal, that allow the radius to rotate around the ulna producing pronation and supination of the forearm.

Bones
Radius and ulna
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Pivot
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Pronation and supination
Clinical Pearl
Nursemaid's elbow
Radial head subluxation, sometimes called nursemaid's elbow or pulled elbow, occurs when a young child's outstretched arm is suddenly pulled. The annular ligament slips over the radial head, trapping it. The child holds the arm pronated and refuses to use it. Reduction is usually accomplished with supination and flexion, often with a satisfying click. The condition is rare after age five as the annular ligament thickens.
Section 3

Joints of the Pelvic Girdle and Lower Limb

Weight-bearing articulations from the pelvis to the ankle, the most heavily loaded joints in the body.

Plate 10

Sacroiliac (SI) Joint

The complex articulation that transmits the entire weight of the upper body from the sacrum to the ilia of the pelvis.

Bones
Ilium and sacrum
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Plane
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis in youth, amphiarthrosis in adults
Movements
Gliding, slight movement
Clinical Pearl
SI joint dysfunction in pregnancy
The hormone relaxin, secreted late in pregnancy, increases ligamentous laxity throughout the pelvis to facilitate childbirth. This includes loosening of the SI joints. Many pregnant women develop posterior pelvic pain or sacroiliac dysfunction, especially in the third trimester. Symptoms typically improve postpartum as relaxin levels fall, but some women develop chronic SI dysfunction.
Plate 11

Pubic Symphysis

The midline cartilaginous joint connecting the two pubic bones across the front of the pelvis.

Bones
Right and left pubic bones
Structural Classification
Cartilaginous
Functional Classification
Symphysis
Movement Classification
Amphiarthrosis
Movements
Slight movement, increases during childbirth
Clinical Pearl
Diastasis of the pubic symphysis
Late in pregnancy and during labor, the interpubic disc softens dramatically under hormonal influence (relaxin). The normal gap of 4 to 5 mm can widen to 9 mm or more. Rarely the joint separates completely (pubic symphysis diastasis), causing severe anterior pelvic pain and difficulty bearing weight. Most cases resolve postpartum with rest, a pelvic binder, and physical therapy.
Plate 12

Acetabulofemoral (Hip) Joint

The deep ball-and-socket joint between the head of the femur and the acetabulum of the coxal bone, sacrificing some mobility for the stability needed to bear weight in upright stance.

Bones
Head of the femur and acetabulum of the coxal bone
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Ball and socket
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, rotation, circumduction
Clinical Pearl
Hip dislocation in seatbelt-era trauma
The hip is the most stable major synovial joint, locked in place by the deep acetabulum, the strong joint capsule, and powerful surrounding muscles. Dislocation requires high-energy trauma. The most common mechanism is a "dashboard injury" in motor vehicle collisions: a flexed and adducted knee strikes the dashboard, driving the femoral head posteriorly out of the acetabulum. Posterior dislocations comprise 90 percent of cases. The sciatic nerve, which runs just behind the hip, is at risk.
Plate 13

Knee Joint

The largest and most complex synovial joint in the body, with weight-bearing femorotibial articulations and a separate patellofemoral joint, all reinforced by extensive ligaments and menisci.

Bones
Femur and tibia (with patella articulating anteriorly at the patellofemoral joint)
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Hinge (modified)
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Flexion and extension, with slight rotation when flexed
Key Structures of the Joint
Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL)
Runs from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial intercondylar area. Resists anterior translation of the tibia on the femur. Most commonly injured in non-contact pivoting injuries with a planted foot.
Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL)
Runs from the medial femoral condyle to the posterior tibial intercondylar area. Resists posterior translation of the tibia on the femur. Often injured in dashboard injuries with knee flexion.
Medial Collateral Ligament (MCL)
Runs from the medial femoral epicondyle to the medial tibia. Resists valgus stress (forces pushing the knee inward). Commonly sprained in skiing and contact sports.
Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL)
Runs from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the head of the fibula. Resists varus stress (forces pushing the knee outward). Less commonly injured than the MCL.
Medial Meniscus
C-shaped fibrocartilage cushioning the medial femorotibial articulation. More commonly torn than the lateral meniscus due to its tighter peripheral attachment to the joint capsule and MCL.
Lateral Meniscus
Smaller, more circular fibrocartilage cushioning the lateral femorotibial articulation. More mobile than the medial meniscus.
Quadriceps Tendon
Connects the quadriceps femoris muscle group to the superior pole of the patella. Transmits the force of knee extension.
Patellar Ligament
The continuation of the quadriceps tendon below the patella, attaching to the tibial tuberosity. Sometimes called the patellar tendon.
Clinical Pearl
The unhappy triad
The classic "unhappy triad" or "O'Donoghue's triad" describes simultaneous injury of the ACL, MCL, and medial meniscus, typically from a lateral blow to a planted, slightly flexed knee. The mechanism creates valgus stress (MCL), rotation with the foot fixed (ACL), and shear on the medial meniscus, which is tethered to the MCL. Modern imaging data suggest the lateral meniscus is actually more often involved than the medial in many ACL injuries.
Plate 14

Tibiofibular Joint

Two articulations between the tibia and fibula. The proximal joint allows slight gliding, and the distal joint forms the syndesmosis essential to ankle stability.

Bones
Tibia and fibula
Structural Classification
Fibrous
Functional Classification
Syndesmosis
Movement Classification
Amphiarthrosis
Movements
Slight movement
Clinical Pearl
Maisonneuve fracture: the missed injury
A Maisonneuve fracture pairs a proximal fibular fracture with a distal tibiofibular syndesmosis disruption and a deltoid ligament tear at the medial ankle. Patients present with pain at the ankle, but the standard ankle X-ray may appear normal. Always palpate the entire fibula in an ankle injury. A missed proximal fibular fracture means a missed unstable ankle.
Plate 15

Talocrural (Ankle) Joint

The hinge joint of the ankle, formed by the trochlea of the talus fitting into the mortise created by the medial malleolus of the tibia and the lateral malleolus of the fibula.

Bones
Talus, tibia, and fibula
Structural Classification
Synovial
Functional Classification
Hinge
Movement Classification
Diarthrosis
Movements
Dorsiflexion and plantarflexion
Clinical Pearl
High ankle sprain vs low ankle sprain
A standard inversion ankle sprain stretches the lateral collateral ligaments below the joint (the anterior talofibular and calcaneofibular ligaments). A high ankle sprain tears the anterior tibiofibular ligament above the joint, disrupting the syndesmosis. High ankle sprains take much longer to heal than low ankle sprains and can require surgical fixation if the syndesmosis is unstable.
BIO 004 Digital Atlas Library

Joints (Articulations)

Fifteen plates organized by region: skull and vertebral column, pectoral girdle and upper limb, pelvic girdle and lower limb. Each plate provides articulating bones, structural and functional classification, range of motion, and a clinical pearl. Print individual plates from your browser for the lab notebook.

Curated by Dr. Sharilyn Rennie
